intake.finding: Clear, bounded problem and owner set. ChromeStatus entry created 2026-02-03 by nrosenthal, component Blink>HTML>Parser, owners nrosenthal+foolip. Motivation is explicitly tied to the parent 'Out of order streaming' work: defining declarative ranges with elements is insufficient, and a 'lighter' non-element node is wanted that does not perturb CSS relationships, with future uses named (CSS custom highlights, HTML response buffering/streaming). The feature_note records that it ships as part of feature 5111042975465472, so the parent/child relationship is explicit at intake.
intake.limitations: ChromeStatus motivation is the team's own framing; no independent problem-validation study is linked.

incubation.finding: Public WICG incubation with a substantive explainer. The patching-explainer.md defines goals, non-goals, multiple rejected alternatives (a `marker` attribute, marker pointers on Element, a `contentmethod` attribute, a new node type), the Self-Review Security/Privacy questionnaire, and integration paths (custom highlights, DOM Parts, script-initiated patching). Initial public proposal at WICG/webcomponents#1116. The parser-level concern (recognising XML PI syntax previously parsed as bogus comments) is surfaced early as the key compat risk. Independent demand for out-of-order streaming predates the proposal (framework interest, community posts).
incubation.limitations: The PI primitive's standalone jobs are mediated through the parent feature; direct developer demand for 'PI parsing' as such is not separately evidenced.

prototype.finding: Concrete prototype/spec work behind a flag with tests and multi-engine implementation bugs. Spec PR #12118 first commit 2026-01-30 carries detailed in-commit rationale (consume exactly the same characters as bogus comments to minimise risk; blocklist `xml`/`xml-stylesheet`; target rules). Tests written at web-platform-tests/wpt#60828. Implementation bugs filed for Chromium (#481087638), Gecko (bugzilla 2046367) and WebKit (317651). The PR's own implementer-interest checklist states 'At least two implementers are interested (and none opposed): Chromium, WebKit.' The Chrome blog reports experimental availability from Chrome 148 behind chrome://flags/#enable-experimental-web-platform-features.
prototype.limitations: No published task-based friction log from the prototype stage; no framework-integration spike results.

developerTrials.finding: Informal developer exposure, not a structured trial. The I2E thread (2026-04-01) records the feature 'enabled as an experimental feature in Canary for a few weeks' with 'no bugs filed on it so far'. The Chrome blog (2026-05-19) invites developer testing from Chrome 148 and ships npm polyfills (template-for-polyfill, html-setters-polyfill) to broaden reach. However there is no cohort, methodology, task-completion, or published friction report. An independent developer (Modern Web Weekly, 2026-06-05) did hit a real friction: the safe `streamAppendHTML()` variant 'results in empty HTML and doesn't work' for templates/markers, forcing the Unsafe variant.
developerTrials.limitations: No attributable developer-trial cohort or published friction findings from the team; the one documented friction comes from an outside developer, not a trial readout.

wideReview.finding: Broad standards review with positive outcomes, on a clear timeline. TAG design-review #1134 (opened 2025-08-12) is closed 'Resolution: satisfied' (CG early review). WHATWG spec PR #12118 was merged by editor annevk on 2026-06-25 (5 days before stable). Mozilla position #1369 = position: positive; WebKit position #628 = position: support. Caveat on timing and attribution: at I2E time (2026-03-31) the same Gecko and WebKit issues were recorded as 'No signal' and TAG as 'Pending'; the positive/support labels crystallised later, and both vendor issues are filed against the parent 'Out of order streaming' feature, not against this PI entry specifically.
wideReview.limitations: Vendor positions are on the parent feature; the vendor issues' comment threads (substantive review content) were not retrievable via the reader, only labels/metadata. At I2E the positions were 'No signal'.

experiment.finding: A field experiment was justified and approved, but no public outcome report exists. The I2E (2026-03-31) explicitly chose a 1%-stable finch (HTMLProcessingInstruction) rather than an Origin Trial, because real-origin exposure was needed to uncover web-compat issues from changing parser behaviour, while the design could still be revised. Mike Taylor asked about fuzzing and Daniel Bratell about canary/beta risk; nrosenthal answered fuzzers 'found some bugs during early development' (since fixed) and that Canary exposure had produced no filed bugs. Yoav Weiss LGTM'd 'to experiment M148-M150 inclusive'. The conservative design (identical character consumption to bogus comments) is the stated compat mitigation. However no public readout of the 1%→100% finch (compat findings, telemetry, holdback comparison) was found; the Chromium tracking bug is not publicly readable.
experiment.limitations: Finch telemetry/readout and the Chromium bug detail are not in public evidence; 'no compat issues' is inferred from absence of filed bugs, not a published result.

prepareToShip.finding: Ship intent and plan exist and the launch executed on schedule, but the authoritative record lagged reality near ship. An Intent to Ship is present (stage_type 160, intent_stage 5; msgid thread URL in the cached record) targeting desktop/android/webview first=150, rollout_plan 'Will ship enabled for all users'. Spec merged 2026-06-25; stable shipped 2026-06-30. However at the ChromeStatus accurate_as_of (2026-06-22) the record still showed browsers.chrome.status='Proposed', is_released=false, and both security_review_status and privacy_review_status='Pending', i.e. the gate/review and release-status fields were stale versus the imminent ship. The I2S thread itself and the Chromium bug could not be retrieved (msgid URL rejected; issues.chromium.org requires auth).
prepareToShip.limitations: Intent-to-Ship thread content, Chromium bug detail, and the actual security/privacy review sign-off state were not retrievable; review-status fields show 'Pending' at the cache date.

release.finding: The feature shipped in Chrome 150 stable on 2026-06-30 across Android, ChromeOS, Linux, macOS, Windows (and WebView; iOS null). The Chrome 150 release notes list both 'Out of order streaming' (bug 431374376) and 'Parse processing instructions in HTML' (bug 481087638) as separate items with accurate descriptions; no deprecations/removals in the release. Communication shipped alongside: official Chrome blog (2026-05-19), MDN/ProcessingInstruction exists, npm polyfills published, independent tutorials and an independent SPA demo. Claims in the release notes match the spec (PI syntax <?target data>, attribute-style API).
release.limitations: ChromeStatus status fields lagged the ship (see prepareToShip); not a release-note error but an authoritative-record freshness gap.

adoption.finding: Too early and no usage evidence. The feature has been stable for ~3 weeks at the evidence cutoff (shipped 2026-06-30). No Chrome UseCounter/HTTP Archive/web-features adoption data is available yet. Adoption-enabling artifacts exist (npm polyfills, demos, tutorials, framework docs for the parent concept in Marko.js) and independent educational coverage exists, but these are enablers/interest, not production adoption. No attributable production deployment was found.
adoption.limitations: Usage data not yet collected/reported at this point in the lifecycle; absence of adoption evidence is expected, not a research failure.

support.finding: Too early to assess. ~3 weeks post-ship. No support-issue taxonomy, known-issues page, or support-burden data found. One real friction is documented by an independent developer (safe streaming variant does not preserve <template>/markers; Unsafe required) which is a candidate support/Docs topic. No reported regressions in the release notes.
support.limitations: No support telemetry or issue volume available; cannot score.

deprecation.finding: Not applicable. This is a new parser capability / new feature launch, not a deprecation, restriction, or removal. There is no removal path, migration, or reverse-trial in scope. (Used per the rule: not-relevant is justified, not a substitute for untested.) The only deprecation-adjacent note is the deliberate non-parsing of `<?xml`/`<?xml-stylesheet>` as bogus comments to preserve existing behaviour.

developerValue.finding: Real, pre-existing developer demand for the capability the primitive enables, but the PI parsing sub-feature is plumbing whose value is mediated through the parent 'Out of order streaming' feature and named future uses (CSS custom highlights, DOM Parts). Demand for out-of-order/declarative streaming is independently evidenced (Marko.js framework docs, community explorations, positive independent press calling it 'huuuuge' and 'revolutionary'). The standalone PI primitive is not directly developer-facing. An independent developer hit a concrete friction (Unsafe streaming required for templates). Value is plausible and motivated but, at ~3 weeks post-ship, unproven in production.
developerValue.limitations: Demand evidence is largely for the parent feature; standalone PI value is inferred from its role as an enabler.

endUserImpact.finding: No measurable end-user impact evidence. The parser change has no direct end-user surface; intended end-user benefit (faster perceived loads via out-of-order streaming) depends on sites adopting the parent feature, which has not yet happened at scale. No CWV/INP/LCP or user-experience measurement is available.
endUserImpact.limitations: End-user impact is wholly indirect and not yet observable; unscored rather than inferred.

adoption.finding: No usage/production-adoption data available. Feature stable since 2026-06-30 (~3 weeks). No UseCounter, HTTP Archive, web-features/Baseline, or partner/production deployment evidence exists yet. Adoption enablers (npm polyfills, demos, tutorials) and framework interest are present but are not adoption.
adoption.limitations: Too early in lifecycle; no adoption instrumentation reported. Unsccored, not failure.

interoperability.finding: Strong standards alignment but Chrome-only implementation at ship. Spec merged into the HTML standard (PR #12118, 2026-06-25) before stable; TAG satisfied; Mozilla positive and WebKit support; tentative WPT test exists (wpt#60828); three-engine implementation bugs filed. However at cutoff only Chromium ships it: WebKit bug 317651 is NEW (filed 2026-06-23, references the merged PR, no implementation) and Gecko bug 2046367 is NEW, UNASSIGNED, flagged behind-pref/firefox-backlog, filed 'mostly to have something to reference for the required Sanitizer API work.' The ChromeStatus ff/safari 'Positive/Support' labels are on the parent OoOS feature and reflect position, not implementation. Baseline (≥2 engines, 30 months) is far off; npm polyfills bridge the gap for now.
interoperability.limitations: Live WPT cross-browser pass/fail not retrievable (JS-rendered dashboard); exact Gecko/WebKit implementation progress beyond bug status unknown.

implementationQuality.finding: Solid compat de-risking and a clean stable ship, but late design churn and no public post-ship telemetry. Compat was de-risked via HTTP Archive analysis ('relatively safe; doesn't parse xml-stylesheet and special characters like $'), fuzzers that 'found some bugs during early development' (fixed), Canary exposure with no filed bugs, then a 1% stable finch; the design deliberately consumes exactly the same characters as bogus comments to minimise parser-change risk. Released in Chrome 150 stable with no regressions listed. Against this: the PI target case-sensitivity flip-flopped during the PR (lowercased 2026-03-18 → 'Don't lowercase PI target' finalised only 2026-06-16, ~2 weeks before stable) and underscore-in-target was allowed 2026-06-15, i.e. the API surface was still being settled as the release train was committed; and no public compat/stability telemetry for the finch or post-ship period was found.
implementationQuality.limitations: Chromium bug/finch telemetry not publicly readable; post-ship stability evidence is absence-of-reported-bugs, not a measured result.

evidenceAndCommunication.finding: Communication was coherent, accurate, and shipped with enablement. Official Chrome blog 'Declarative partial updates' (Barry Pollard + Noam Rosenthal, 2026-05-19), accurate Chrome 150 release-notes entries for both the PI feature and the parent OoOS feature, MDN ProcessingInstruction, and npm polyfills (template-for-polyfill, html-setters-polyfill) all describe consistent behaviour matching the merged spec. Claims match actual rollout (M150, all desktop+android+webview, no iOS). One data-quality caveat: the ChromeStatus authoritative record lagged the ship (status='Proposed', reviews='Pending', is_released=false at the 2026-06-22 accurate_as_of, despite a 2026-06-30 stable ship and 2026-06-25 spec merge) — a record-freshness gap, not a messaging error.
evidenceAndCommunication.limitations: ChromeStatus status/review fields were stale at the cache date; scored on the public communication artifacts, which were accurate.

support.finding: No support evidence yet (~3 weeks post-ship). No known-issues material, support-volume data, or issue taxonomy found. One candidate support/docs topic is documented externally: the safe streaming variant does not preserve templates/markers, so the Unsafe variant is required. No reported post-ship regressions.
support.limitations: No support telemetry available; cannot score.

overall.finding: A well-run, standards-first launch of a foundational parser primitive that shipped cleanly with strong cross-engine signals and good DevRel enablement — but real developer/end-user value, adoption, and interoperability remain unproven. Strengths: spec merged before stable, TAG satisfied, Mozilla positive + WebKit support, conservative compat design, fuzzing + Canary + 1% finch de-risking, npm polyfills and docs shipped alongside. Open/mixed: Chrome-only in practice (Gecko unassigned/behind-pref, WebKit bug NEW), the PI value is mediated through the parent OoOS feature, the API surface was still churning ~2 weeks pre-ship, the authoritative ChromeStatus record lagged the launch, and adoption/usage/support are not yet observable at ~3 weeks post-ship. Overall is deliberately mixed; it does not hide the unscored adoption/end-user dimensions or the Chrome-only interop reality.
overall.limitations: Post-ship outcome dimensions (adoption, end-user impact, support) are too early to evidence; the report cannot assert success or failure on them.

sources.limitations: Thread text captured; linked Intent-to-Prototype msgid and the Intent-to-Ship msgid thread were not separately retrievable.

supports: Chrome 150 stable release date 2026-06-30; lists both 'Parse processing instructions in HTML' (bug 481087638) and 'Out of order streaming' (bug 431374376) with accurate descriptions; no deprecations/removals in release

sources.publisher: Chrome for Developers (Barry Pollard, Noam Rosenthal)

sources.limitations: Published before spec merge (2026-06-25) and stable ship (2026-06-30); says standards 'in the process' at the time.

supports: Independent positive community coverage with a built SPA demo; confirms 'WebKit announced support, Firefox positive'; documents a real developer friction: safe streamAppendHTML() 'results in empty HTML and doesn't work', Unsafe variant required for templates/markers
sources.limitations: Independent single-author newsletter; one data point on developer friction.

supports: Framework (Marko.js) documents out-of-order streaming concept, signalling ecosystem demand for the parent capability
sources.limitations: Retrieved as search snippet only; full page not read; about the concept, not this specific API.

supports: Independent educational/tutorial coverage of declarative partial updates including processing-instruction placeholders
sources.limitations: Retrieved as search snippet only; full article not read; publication date unknown.

supports: Tentative WPT test parse-processing-instruction.tentative.html authored alongside the spec (referenced from PR #12118)
sources.limitations: PR read failed (provider error); live wpt.fyi dashboard is JS-rendered and returned no pass/fail data; cross-browser results not retrieved.

successes: Standards-first sequencing: WICG explainer → WHATWG spec PR #12118 merged by editor (annevk) on 2026-06-25, before the 2026-06-30 stable ship; TAG design-review #1134 satisfied; Mozilla positive and WebKit support; tests written and implementation bugs filed in all three engines. [s_spec, s_tag, s_moz, s_webkit, s_wpt]

Conservative, well-de-risked parser change: HTTP Archive analysis ('relatively safe'), fuzzing that found and fixed early-development bugs, multi-week Canary exposure with no filed bugs, then a 1%-stable finch (HTMLProcessingInstruction) before full ship; the design consumes exactly the same characters as the prior bogus-comment behaviour to minimise web-compat risk. [s_i2e, s_spec, s_cs]

DevRel enablement shipped with the platform feature: official Chrome blog (2026-05-19), accurate Chrome 150 release notes, and npm polyfills (template-for-polyfill, html-setters-polyfill) so developers can use the capability cross-browser before Gecko/WebKit ship. [s_blog, s_relnotes]
Spec carries in-commit rationale and rejected alternatives, easing multi-engine review. [s_spec, s_explainer]
failures: ChromeStatus authoritative record lagged the launch: at the 2026-06-22 accurate_as_of it showed browsers.chrome.status='Proposed', is_released=false, security_review_status='Pending', privacy_review_status='Pending' — despite the spec merging 2026-06-25 and stable shipping 2026-06-30. Anyone using ChromeStatus as the launch readout would have been misled about release/review state. [s_cs vs s_spec/s_relnotes]

API surface was still churning ~2 weeks before stable: PI target case-sensitivity changed repeatedly (lowercased 2026-03-18 → 'Don't lowercase PI target' finalised 2026-06-16) and underscore-in-target allowed 2026-06-15, signalling the design was not fully settled when the release train committed. [s_spec]

Interop is Chrome-only at ship despite the 'Positive/Support' labels: Gecko bug is NEW/UNASSIGNED/behind-pref (filed as a reference for Sanitizer API work), WebKit bug is NEW with no patch. The ChromeStatus ff/safari positions are filed against the parent feature and reflect position, not implementation — a real risk of overstating cross-browser availability. [s_gecko_bug, s_webkit_bug, s_moz, s_webkit, s_cs]

No published finch readout or post-ship compat/stability telemetry: 'no compat issues' rests on absence of filed bugs and the Chromium bug is not publicly readable, so the compat outcome is asserted rather than evidenced. [s_i2e, s_cs]
counterfactuals: If the spec had merged a cycle earlier (it merged 2026-06-25, five days before stable), the launch would not have shipped under a 'standards maturity: Working draft / under development' label, removing ambiguity about spec stability at ship.
If a published 1%→100% finch readout existed, the 'no web-compat issues' claim would be defensible public evidence instead of an inference from absence of bug reports.

If implementation status (engine bug state) rather than standards position were the tracked interop metric, the 'Positive/Support' labels would not have risked over-representing cross-browser availability at launch.

If a short, published developer-trial friction report existed, the externally-discovered friction (safe streaming variant drops templates/markers; Unsafe required) could have been addressed in official docs before broad release.
If the parent/child feature relationship were surfaced in the launch readout, the foundational PI primitive would not be scored as if it were the directly user-facing capability.
skillImprovements: Add a retrospective rule: score interoperability from engine IMPLEMENTATION status (bug NEW/assigned/landed/shipped), never from a positive/support standards position alone; require an implementation-status check per engine and label position-vs-implementation distinctly.

Add a phase-04/06 freshness check: flag when ChromeStatus status / *_review_status / is_released fields lag the actual milestone ship by more than N days, so a stale authoritative record is not mistaken for the launch state.
Add a 'spec maturity at ship date' checkpoint: capture whether the normative spec is merged before stable, record the merge-commit timestamp, and treat same-week merges as a risk flag.
Require counterfactual evidence for compat claims: 'no compat issues' must cite a finch/telemetry readout or HTTP Archive result, not absence of bug reports.

Add a parent/child feature handling rule: when a sub-feature ships as part of a parent (e.g. PI parsing within Out of order streaming), capture the relationship and avoid scoring the plumbing primitive as the directly user-facing capability, and avoid double-counting shared evidence.
Add a late-design-churn detector: flag substantive API-surface changes (e.g. case-sensitivity, allowed characters) within ~2 weeks of the stable branch cut as a prepare-to-ship risk.

missingEvidence: Intent to Ship thread content (msgid URL rejected by the reader) and the Intent to Prototype thread detail
Chromium tracking bug #481087638 detail and the 1%→100% finch/telemetry readout (issues.chromium.org requires auth; not public)
Live WPT cross-browser pass/fail for parse-processing-instruction (dashboard is JS-rendered; PR read failed) and whether the .tentative test was de-tentative after spec merge
Post-ship adoption/usage data and production deployments — not yet collected at ~3 weeks post stable (2026-06-30)
Post-ship support issue volume/known-issues — not yet observable at ~3 weeks post stable
TAG/Mozilla/WebKit reviewer comment reasoning (only labels/metadata were retrievable)
